sentences.text: My name is Erna Roth Anolik.
E-R-N-A, Erna.
Roth, R-O-T-H.
And Anolik is A-N-O-L-I-K.
No, I don't.
Well, actually Erna is short for Ernestine.
So everybody calls me Erna.
December 15, 1923.
73. 72, actually.
Want to be right.
[LAUGHS]
U-C-H-O-R-O-D.
Father's name was Ignac Roth.
I-G-N-A-C, Ignac.
Roth, R-O-T-H.
In the Austro-Hungarian Empire, but I cannot tell you what town.
Yes.
I guess equivalent to high school.
That was as high as he went.
He was a wine merchant.
But as a young man, he worked in a bank.
And then he went into business for himself, and he was a wine merchant.
I wouldn't know.
[LAUGHS]
Never talked about it, so I don't know.
Was he ever a member of any political party?
No, he was not.
No.
Jewish.
Orthodox.
Maria Lebovitz Roth.
M-A-R-I-A.
Lebovitz is L-E-B-O-V-I-T-- T-Z or sometimes it's spelled with C.
I'm not-- and Roth, R-O-T-H.
She was born in Szeredne in the Austro-Hungarian Empire also.
S-Z-E-R-E-D-N-E.
No.
That was a small town.
It was a near Uchorod.
Yes.
But I, I can't really tell you how far she has gone to school.
No.
No.
No.
No.
She was not.
Jewish.
Again, orthodox.
Yeah.
Yes.
I had-- my oldest brother was Joseph Roth.
Then I had a sister, Olga, another sister, Elizabeth, and a younger brother Erwin.
He was born in Uchorod on January 7th, 1918.
Yes.
Yes, of course.
He finished high school.
No, he did not.
No, he did not.
He went to work--
--after high school.
Yeah.
Worked, worked as a salesman in a department store.
I think it was called Gerstli, G-E-R-S-T-L-I.
I think that was--
Yes.
It was not quite as large.
It was probably on the smaller scale, but was a department store, nevertheless, you know?
No.
No.
Well, he left home in 1938 to go to Israel.
So he survived in Israel.
In fact, he just died one month ago today.
Yeah.
She was born October 16th, 1919 in Uchorod.
And she also finished high school, and then she went on to learn a-- sewing, to be a seamstress.
Yes, she did.
She married in 1941 in Uchorod.
Elizabeth was born August 7th or 9th-- and-- I think it was-- must have been 9th, in-- in 1921 in Uchorod.
And she finished high school as well.
And as my older sister, she, after high school, she went to learn sewing, you know, to be a seamstress.
No.
No, she did not.
Yes.
He was born May 26, 1932 in Uchorod.
No.
No, she was not.
Yes.
She was with me all the time, through the concentration camp.
No.
Mhm.
Well, Uchorod was the capital of the Carpathian part of Czechoslovakia, which is the eastern most part of Czechoslovakia.
And it was-- I, I think I had a wonderful childhood.
We had a lot of friends.
A lot of our relatives lived in town, also my father's family mainly.
My father had one sister who lived in the same town-- same street as we did.
And then we had-- he had two younger brothers who lived in town.
My aunt, who lived in the same street as we did, had children very close to our-- my, my age and my sisters ages, brothers ages.
So we saw them very often.
And I was very friendly with my cousin, who now lives in Pittsburgh.
And even in school, I remember a very happy childhood.
Had a lot of friends, and parents were quite religious.
I remember, for instance, having to always be home on Shabbat for our dinner meal.
We have to be there at home.
My father came home from the synagogue, and we all had dinner together.
And what else shall I tell you about it?
And then I went to the gymnasium and-- in fact, in 1939 or '40, the Hungarians occupied that part of Czechoslovakia.
And then I had to transfer from the Czech gymnasium to the Hebrew gymnasium.
And so I developed a whole new set of friends, again, in a new school.
And it was very pleasant, very nice.
But then-- and we were just graduating from the Gymnasium in April 24th, 1944 when there was a, a proclamation.
All Jews have to leave home the very next day.
So we could not finish our graduation, and we left home the very next day.
OK.
Yep.
Yes.
It was Eight Bozos Utca.
Bozos is spelled B-O-Z-O-S.
Utca means street, and utca is spelled U-T-C-A.
And that was in Uchorod.
No.
It was an apartment and there were two apartments in the house.
And then there was a courtyard.
And behind, there were, again, the landlord lived behind, also in an apartment.
And we lived there until I was 15 years old.
And then in 1938, we moved to another apartment temporarily while my parents had a house built, which we moved into about 1939.
Well, since all my friends were Jewish-- until fairly recently, when somebody asked me, I said, at least half the town had to be Jewish, and then I found out it wasn't so.
It just seemed to me that way because all my friends and everybody I associated with were Jewish.
But I think about 30% of the town must have been Jewish.
None.
None at all.
Never.
Never.
Yeah.
Yeah.
My closest friend-- cousin was Piri, Piri Weinberger.
We went to school together and we used to play around after school, hang around together.
And she now lives in Pittsburgh, and we still are very close.
Yes.
I remember my-- the friends from later years, from the Hebrew gymnasium.
And a couple of them survived, and I'm still very close to them.
My very good friend is Susan White, who now lives in Englewood, New Jersey-- Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey.
Then there's Edith Ellis, and she lives in West Orange, New Jersey.
So I'm very close to them.
Then there's another friend in California, who I see occasionally.
But that's, that's about it.
Yeah.
And there's other ones that survived.
I always liked math.
I always liked math, and I also liked geography.
I, generally-- I enjoyed school a lot.
Well, that-- that I thought of much later on, when I was in a gymnasium, and I wanted to be a textile engineer.
And it so happened that's what I did when I came to the States, became a textile engineer.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Generally, when I think of, of home, of my parents' house, and-- [COUGHS] Excuse me.
Those are times, I remember, that we always-- the Shabbat-- being always together and being having dinners always together.
And the holidays were very significant.
My parents were orthodox, so we always observed every holiday.
My father went to synagogue every day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I remember also having friends in the-- in the building that we lived in.
The landlord had a-- a daughter exactly my age.
She went to school with me also.
They were Hasidic.
And I remember, I could go-- sometime I would go to their house on Shabbat or on holidays.
And when I went there, you could only speak Yiddish, if you went to their house on holidays, especially when her father was home.
You have to, had to speak Yiddish.
And I didn't speak Yiddish too well, so I would go there but not very often on holidays or on Shabbat.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
As I said, we observed all the holidays.
We observed strictly, and I-- I would go-- what I remember about holidays, for instance, that on Tish'a B'Av, we used to go to my very religious friends.
And we used to sit there on the floor and pray and-- certain things come back, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We certainly-- because the families were so large-- like, for instance, Passover, over here, the whole family gets together and a lot of people.
We didn't do that, I guess because we lived in apartments and the apartments were not large enough to accommodate very large families.
So for, for Passover, we just had immediate family together.
And then after the dinner, we would go visiting the relatives or they would come to our house.
And a lot of my father's family, and as well as my mother's family, they lived in, in small towns.
So when they came into the city to visit, they would always stay with us.
And that was very pleasant.
Nice memories for that.

And on the other hand, on-- in the summers when we were on school vacations, I used to go either to my grandparents-- my father's parents-- for a vacation, or sometime to some of my aunts in Szeredne.
But I especially remember enjoying very much going to visit my grandparents, because they had a farm and they grew their own vegetables, and fruits, and seeing them milk cows.
So it was exciting for me.
I used to enjoy going there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Yes.
My grandfather's name was Herman Roth, and my grandmother's name was, I think it was, Rebecca Roth.
And my father's family was a very large family.
He had a-- he had three brothers, and must have been at least six or eight sisters.
So.
Yes.
The aunt who lived in town was Ida.
We called her Idushka.
And then there was a brother, David, another brother [?
Bela-- ?] which is William.
And those are the ones who lived in town.
Then there was one aunt on my mother's side that I was very close to because my mother came from a very large family.
I didn't even know all the aunts and uncles.
But I think that most of them were half sisters and brothers to my mother.
There was one real sister, and we were very close to her.
Her name was Dora Birnbaum.
And she lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
And she also had daughters very close to my age.
In fact, her-- she had two daughters.
The younger one has the same name as I have, and she's very-- I'm even between the two girls so we were very close also.
Not too many toys.
[LAUGHS] We just had to play tag or whatever.
But we were mostly playing outside in the yard or whatever.
In the winter, we went sleigh riding.
We-- in the summer, we were at the river swimming all day long, all afternoon definitely.
We spent a lot of time at on at the river.
But there weren't too many toys.
[LAUGHS] Nobody had too many toys.
But I do remember when somebody would come and visit us with a bicycle.
I was very anxious to learn to ride a bicycle.
So my father would ask whoever's bicycle it was if he could teach me to ride a bicycle.
And I learned to ride a bicycle.
I was very happy about that.
And as a result now, my whole family's bicycle riders.
[LAUGHS]
Well, in Czechoslovakia, religion was one of the subjects you learned in school.
It was-- we went to school from 8:00 to 1:00, and then any-- like gym and religion was taught after the one o'clock class.
We'd go back for those classes.
So religion was taught in school.
And it was not taught-- in the-- the Jewish kids went to the Jewish religion class.
Catholics would probably go to the Catholic religion, or Protestants to the Protestants.
So we were not, the whole group, learning religion together.
So we had religion there.
And, of course, in my family, as, as early as we could learn, we had to learn prayer in the morning and prayer at night.
And-- and then later on, I went to the Hebrew gymnasium.
So there, I had religion again.
There was much more intense.
I learned Torah and [YIDDISH].
And-- so I definitely had to learn religion.
Yeah.
You mentioned your synagogue.
Do you remember the name of the synagogue?
Did it have a name?
I don't remember.
I think it was just named after the street that it was on.
And it was the Radvanc.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
R-A-D-V-A-N-C.
Radvanc.
No, I don't.
I don't remember.
Mhm.
The name of the river was Uz.
U-Z.
That's why the name was called Uchorod, because it was named after the river.
And also there was a, a castle just above the river and the name horod mean-- I think it's in Russian-- or in Czech-- meant castle.
So castle on the Uz, on the river Uz.
And that's where the name of the town came from.
Uchorod.
Yeah.
My father was a wine merchant, and mother, my mother was just homemaker.
Yeah.
We had a maid who was-- who lived with us.
Oh, she had the house cleaning and laundry.
That was mainly it because my mother did all the cooking.
But the-- the maid did the house cleaning and the laundry, ironing, that, that type of thing.
Mhm.
The Apartment.
It was on the street that the main market was on.
The, the market-- we didn't have big supermarkets like we have here.
So, for instance, produce and chickens, geese, ducks, and all these things, you bought-- the poultry, you bought it live at the market.
So people from surrounding towns-- farmers-- used to bring their produce and livestock into town every day, but especially on Thursdays, I think.
So the market was set up right in our street, on Bozos Utca.
The produce, farmers were mainly Bulgarians.
And-- so they-- they would bring in the produce and fruits and, and vegetables.
So we would buy it-- go to the market and buy it all the time.
And that was set up right in front of our house.
They would be out there in the morning.
By twelve or one o'clock in the afternoon, it was all cleaned up.
You wouldn't know there was a market there.
And-- but-- and also, the street we lived on was a continuation of Masaryk [?
Tere. ?] Masaryk was the president of Czechoslovakia and the main-- main square in town was named after him.
It was like a plaza.
Tere ?] means plaza.
So the street I lived on was sort of a continuation of the Masaryk [?
Tere, ?] of the main plaza.
There was a park there with a statue of the president, Masaryk.
And our-- my elementary school was right around the, the same plaza that I went to.
And next door to us was a nunnery.
That was one of our preoccupations was [INAUDIBLE] to always go spy on the nuns.
He had a wine cellar, and he sold mainly wholesale.
But a lot of Jewish-- on Fridays, a lot of Jewish people came to buy wine from him just for Fridays, because it had to be bought from a Jewish person.
Once a non-Jewish person touched a wine, it was no longer kosher.
So a lot of Jewish people came on Fridays.
But mainly, he sold wholesale to inns and-- I guess mainly for inns.
Yeah.
And he would-- he would have-- he had a wine cellar.
The wine was kept in huge barrels.
And I remember in, in the summer, or rather, in the fall, they would harvest the grape, and they would-- he would-- he would make the wine out of it.
And I very fondly remember having the new wine before it fermented.
And we'd go roast potatoes in the skin and have the-- this new wine with it, which was delicious.
Yes.
We spoke Hungarian, because my father mainly spoken Hungarian.
So in the house, actually, at home we spoke mainly Hungarian.
Czech was spoken in school and, and Hungarian at home.
And if my parents didn't want us to understand what they said, then they spoke Yiddish.
